coredata.dc:title: Unique insights into photocatalytic VOCs oxidation over WO3/carbon dots nanohybrids assisted by water activation and electron transfer at interfaces
coredata.dc:creator: Huang, Guimei
Liu, Lijun
Chen, Lv
Gao, Lingfeng
Zhu, Junjiang
Fu, Hongbo
coredata.dc:description: Internal electric field (IEF) at heterojunction interfaces can separate photoexcited charge carriers and promote photocatalytic performance. Here we have modified WO3 nanoplates with carbon dots (CDs) and constructed an interfacial IEF directing from CDs to WO3 with assistance of their remarkably different work functions. Such electric field drove photoexcited electrons to transport towards CDs and retained photoexcited holes to stay at WO3, achieving electron/hole spatial separation. H2O preferred chemisorption on the five-coordinated W atoms of WO3 with an elongated H-O bond and bent H-O-H angle, which allowed the activation of H2O and favorable production of ·OH radicals. The WO3/CDs (WC1) showed a superior photocatalytic activity for visible-light photooxidation of HCHO and CH3COCH3 with CO2 production rate of 411 and 188 μmol g–1 h–1, respectively, outperforming most of WO3-based photocatalysts. The enhanced photocatalytic performance correlated with the IEF-induced charge separation, favorable ·OH production and VOCs chemisorption. Our work confirms the role of CDs cocatalyst in the photocatalytic oxidation of VOCs, which will inspire enthusiasm to develop more advanced heterojunction photocatalysts involving carbon nanomaterials.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Volatile organic compounds
Tungsten oxide
Water activation
Electron/hole separation
Photodegradation

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of WO3 and WCx nanoplates

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Hubei Key Laboratory of Biomass Fibers and Eco-dyeing & Finishing
Outstanding Young and Middle-Aged Scientific and Technological Innovation Team of the Education Department of Hubei Province
Special Project of the Central Committee Guides Local Science and Technology Development of Hubei Province
Hubei Provincial Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Natural Science Foundation of Hubei Province

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by Hubei Provincial Natural Science Foundation of China ( 2020CFB785 ), Science and Technology Research Project of the Education Department of Hubei Province ( D20191703 , Q20181701 ), Project of Hubei Key Laboratory of Biomass Fibers and Eco-dyeing & Finishing (WTU) ( STRZ2020029 ), Special Project of the Central Committee Guides Local Science and Technology Development of Hubei Province ( 2019ZYYD073 ), and the Outstanding Young and Middle-Aged Scientific and Technological Innovation Team of the Education Department of Hubei Province ( T2020011 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD patterns of WO3 nanoplates and different WCx (x devotes to 0.3, 0.7, 1 or 2) nanohybrids. The vertical lines represent the JCPDS #43-1035 datafile of WO3.

(a) TEM image of as-synthesized graphite CDs. Insets are the HRTEM image of an individual CD (top-right) and UV–vis and photoluminescence (PL) spectra of CDs solution (bottom-right). TEM image (b) and SAED pattern (c) of pure WO3 nanoplates. TEM image (d), HRTEM image (e), EDX spectrum (f), and STEM-HAADF image and elemental mappings (g) of the WC1 sample.
The FTIR spectra of CDs, WO3 and different WCx (x devotes to 0.3, 0.7, 1 or 2) nanohybrids.
(a) UV–Vis diffuse reflectance spectra and (b) N2 sorption isotherms and pore size distributions (inset) of WO3 and WCx (x = 0.3, 0.7, 1 or 2) nanohybrids.
(a) Mott-Schottky curves recorded with different frequencies and (b) Valence band XPS spectra of the WO3 nanoplates.

EPR spectra of WO3 and WC1 dispersed in (a) water and (b) methanol with DMPO as radical trapping agent. (c) The kinetic plots and (d) degradation rates of the photocatalytic RhB degradation over WC1 with different radical scavengers. c(RhB) = 5 mg L−1, 20 mg of WC1, V = 50 mL, irradiation time = 30 min.

(a) Profile of the plane-averaged differential charge density and (b) differential charge density (∆ρ) isosurfaces of WO3 (001) surface adsorbed with H2O. Grey, red and pink denote the W, O and H, respectively. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

The optimized adsorption structures of (a) CH3COCH3 and (b) HCHO adsorbed on WO3 (001) surface. Grey, red and pink denote the W, O and H, respectively. (c) Projected crystal orbital Hamilton population (pCOHP) diagram of the W-O linkage for the CH3COCH3-WO3 (001) adsorption structure. Fermi energy levels are set to zero. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Static electronic potentials of (a) CDs (001) and (b) WO3 (001) slabs. Insets are the corresponding atomic configurations of the slabs. (c) O 1s XPS spectrum of WC1. (d) In-situ and ex-situ W 4f XPS spectra of WO3 and WC1.
Schematic illustration of the charge separation of WCx during photocatalytic degradation reaction.

Transient photocurrent response(a), EIS Nyquist plots (b) and photoluminescence spectra (c) of WCx (x = 0.3, 0.7, 1 or 2) nanohybrids and WO3. Inset of panel (b) is the equivalent-circuit diagram. CPE, R s and R ct denote as the solution resistance, charge transfer resistance, and constant phase element, respectively. (d) Time-resolution photoluminescence spectra of WC1 and WO3 at an emission wavelength of 375 nm.
The CO2 production rate of visible-light photocatalytic oxidation of VOCs (a) in air with various WCx (x = 0.3, 0.7, 1 or 2) samples and (b) WC1 with different O2 concentrations.

head.ce:title: Unique insights into photocatalytic VOCs oxidation over WO3/carbon dots nanohybrids assisted by water activation and electron transfer at interfaces

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Hubei Key Laboratory of Biomass Fibers and Eco-dyeing & Finishing, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Wuhan Textile University, Wuhan 430200, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Hubei Key Laboratory of Biomass Fibers and Eco-dyeing & Finishing, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Wuhan Textile University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Hubei Key Laboratory of Biomass Fibers and Eco-dyeing & Finishing, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Wuhan Textile University, Wuhan 430200, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Shanghai Key Laboratory of Atmospheric Particle Pollution and Prevention, Department of Environmental Science & Engineering, Institute of Atmospheric Sciences, Fudan University, Shanghai 200433, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Shanghai Key Laboratory of Atmospheric Particle Pollution and Prevention, Department of Environmental Science & Engineering, Institute of Atmospheric Sciences, Fudan University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Shanghai Key Laboratory of Atmospheric Particle Pollution and Prevention, Department of Environmental Science & Engineering, Institute of Atmospheric Sciences, Fudan University, Shanghai, 200433, P.R. China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Internal electric field (IEF) at heterojunction interfaces can separate photoexcited charge carriers and promote photocatalytic performance. Here we have modified WO3 nanoplates with carbon dots (CDs) and constructed an interfacial IEF directing from CDs to WO3 with assistance of their remarkably different work functions. Such electric field drove photoexcited electrons to transport towards CDs and retained photoexcited holes to stay at WO3, achieving electron/hole spatial separation. H2O preferred chemisorption on the five-coordinated W atoms of WO3 with an elongated H-O bond and bent H-O-H angle, which allowed the activation of H2O and favorable production of ·OH radicals. The WO3/CDs (WC1) showed a superior photocatalytic activity for visible-light photooxidation of HCHO and CH3COCH3 with CO2 production rate of 411 and 188 μmol g–1 h–1, respectively, outperforming most of WO3-based photocatalysts. The enhanced photocatalytic performance correlated with the IEF-induced charge separation, favorable ·OH production and VOCs chemisorption. Our work confirms the role of CDs cocatalyst in the photocatalytic oxidation of VOCs, which will inspire enthusiasm to develop more advanced heterojunction photocatalysts involving carbon nanomaterials.

ce:section.ce:para: Volatile organic compounds (VOCs) are hazardous in air, and some may be toxic, mutagenic, and teratogenic. Continuing emission of VOCs is responsible for the increased ozone concentration and even photochemical smog in atmosphere. Photocatalytic degradation of VOCs under visible light is an attractive and sustainable strategy for air purification, which has encouraged much enthusiasm to develop advanced photocatalysts for VOCs removal (He et al., 2019; Guo et al., 2021; Chen et al., 2021; M. Wang et al., 2021; J. Li et al., 2020; S. Zhang et al., 2020; Zhang et al., 2021; Xu et al., 2021; Gao et al., 2018). As a visible-light responsive photocatalyst, WO3 shows great potential in photodegradation due to its intriguing merits of light absorption up to 500 nm (band gap ~2.5 eV), fast electron transport (∼12 cm2 V–1 s–1), long hole diffusion length, and stability in acidic and oxidative conditions (Wang et al., 2020; Z. Wang et al., 2021; Fu et al., 2019; Albanese et al., 2017). Besides, WO3 has low valance band position (~2.4 eV vs. NHE), enabling it to be an oxidation photocatalyst. The holes of WO3 are able to oxide adsorbed water to produce hydroxyl radicals (·OH), a kind of reactive oxygen species (ROS) for photooxidation (Gao et al., 2020). However, like other nanocomponent photocatalysts, WO3 still suffers from its high electron/hole recombination rate (Q. Xu et al., 2020; Li et al., 2021; Hu et al., 2018; Song et al., 2017). An electron-cocatalyst can capture photoinduced electrons from WO3 matrix and also function as active sites for subsequent redox reaction, promoting the photocatalytic performance (Tomita et al., 2016; Xu et al., 2019b; Y. Li et al., 2020). Noble metal cocatalysts have widely been used to accelerate the separation of photocarriers, but their cost and scarcity impede scalable usages (Xu et al., 2017; T. Xu et al., 2020; Kim et al., 2016). As a result, exploring earth-abundant and cost-effective cocatalysts to meet the growing requirements for VOCs photodegradation is still challenging.

Graphite carbon dots (CDs) are an emerging carbon material with outstanding optical properties, conductivity, stability, nontoxicity and cheapness (Qian et al., 2016; Sun et al., 2021; Mahmood et al., 2021; Kumar et al., 2018; Sharma et al., 2018, 2020b, 2020a). CDs contain sp 2-hybridized carbon and conjugated π-electrons in layer. The delocalized π-electrons allow CDs to have lower work function (~4.5 eV) than most oxide semiconductors (~6 eV in general) (Fernando et al., 2015). Owing to work function difference, CDs could donate electrons to WO3 upon their hybridization and create an internal electric field (IEF) at interfaces between CDs and WO3 (Xiong et al., 2020; Zhao et al., 2019). The electric field has been regarded as a powerful force to drive photoexcited electrons to move along its direction (holes against the direction), achieving electron/hole separation (Lin et al., 2020; Xu et al., 2018b; Wei et al., 2021; Z. Zhang et al., 2020). Apart from the IEF-induced charge separation, the graphite CDs could lower charge transfer resistance and accelerate the migration of photoexcited charge carriers owing to their conductivity (Cheng et al., 2021). All the merits suggest CDs would be a competent electron-cocatalyst and assist WO3 photocatalyst to achieve efficient charge separation and enhanced photocatalytic activity.

Herein, CDs-modified WO3 rectangle nanoplates were prepared to explore the driving force of photoexcited charge separation and the mechanism of photocatalytic oxidation of formaldehyde and acetone. In-situ X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (in-situ XPS) combined with density functional theory (DFT) calculations was utilized to understand the electron transfer at WO3/CDs interfaces and the formation mechanism of IEF. The hydroxide radicals (·OH) were the dominant oxidative species for VOCs photodegradation owing to the overwhelming water activation over WO3. The WO3/CDs nanohybrids showed promising photocatalytic activity for the photodegradation, ascribed to the efficient charge separation, favorable·OH production, and chemisorption of VOCs. This work affirms that CDs are a competent electron-cocatalyst for metal oxide photocatalysts, which might broaden applications of carbon materials in environmental photocatalysis.

All chemicals were of analytical grade purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Company. CDs were synthesized via high-temperature reflux of sodium citrate aqueous solution under atmospheric pressure. Specifically, 0.5 g of trisodium citrate was dissolved into 15 mL of water in a flask and heated to 200 °C in an oil bath. A reflux condenser was equipped to condense the steam into liquid. Prior to heating, the flask was purged with high-purity Ar for 30 min and sealed with a balloon in the condenser outlet. The solution changed from colorless to pale yellow, and then orange within 30 min. The pH of as-obtained solution was then adjusted to 7.0 with use of 10 mg mL–1 of NaOH aqueous solution. The obtained CDs dispersion were purified with a dialysis membrane (3k Da) and stored at 4 °C for further use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of WO3 and WCx nanoplates
ce:section.ce:para: WO3 rectangle nanoplates were synthesized via a hydrothermal and post-calcination process. Brieﬂy, 3.6 mmol of Na2WO6.6 H2O and 3.6 mL of 40 wt% HBF4 were dissolved into 60 mL of deionized water. The solution was transferred into a 100 mL Teﬂon-lined autoclave and heated at 100 ℃ for 12 h. The collected solid precursor was calcinated at 500 °C in atmosphere for 2 h with a heating rate of 2 °C min–1 to form WO3 rectangle nanoplates. To prepare WO3/CDs samples, 0.1 g WO3 nanoplates and a given amount of CDs solution (1 mg mL–1) were ultrasonically dispersed in 5 mL of deionized H2O. The dispersion was magnetically stirred for 24 h to make sure that WO3 nanoplates had sufficient time to adsorb CDs. The solid was then collected by centrifugation. The colorless supernatant suggested that CDs were completely coupled with WO3 nanoplates. As-obtained WO3/CDs was heated at 150 ℃ for 12 h to assure the intimate interfacial contact.

The samples were fully characterized with multiple techniques, which were detailed and presented in Supplementary Material. For the purpose of brevity, the WO3/CDs is denoted as WCx, where W and C represent WO3 and CDs respectively; x is the weight percentage of CDs.

Typically, 20 mg of photocatalysts and 20 mL of deionized H2O were ultrasonically mixed in a two-neck quartz reactor (200 mL) and heated at 105 °C for 2 h to completely evaporate the water. A uniform photocatalyst film was formed at the bottom. The reactor was purged with O2/N2 with different O2 concentration for 0.5 h and sealed with rubber stoppers. Then, 40 μL of 37 wt% HCHO solution (or 20 μL of liquid CH3COCH3) and 200 μL of H2O were injected into the reactor with a microsyringe and allowed to vaporize at 50 °C. After reaching adsorption equilibrium in dark for 2 h, the sample was irradiated with a 300 W xenon lamp (PLS-SXE300D, Beijing Perfectlight, China) with a 15 A output current and UV-cutoff filter (λ > 420 nm). The system was maintained at 30 ℃ with circulating water. The light intensity was 400 mW cm−2 determined by an optical power meter (PL-MW2000, Beijing Perfectlight, China). The product was quantified by a gas chromatograph (GC-9790 plus, Fuli) equipped with methane reformer, two flame ionization detectors, TDX-01 packed column and SE-54 capillary column.

DFT calculations were performed with Vienna Ab initio Simulation Package (VASP) with a projector augmented-wave (PAW) method (Blochl, 1994). Generalized gradient approximation (GGA) with Perdew-Burke-Ernzerhof (PBE) functional was used to describe exchange-correlation energy (Perdew et al., 1996). The energy cutoff was set as 520 eV. The monoclinic WO3 (001)-p(√2 × √2)R45° surface slab was built since the reconstructed surface was the most stable one as revealed by previous theoretic calculation and experimental investigation (Ping et al., 2015). The thickness of vacuum space was set to 20 Å. For geometric optimization, the top half of atoms of the slab was allowed to relax with the energy and force convergence criteria setting to 10–5 eV and 0.02 eV Å–1. A 2 × 2 × 1 Monkhorst-Pack schemed k -mesh was used to sample the first Brillouin zone. For static calculations, a denser k -point grid of 3 × 3 × 1 was set and the energy convergence criterion was set to 10−6 eV. The adsorption energy (E ad) is expressed as E ad = E slab+adsorbate – E slab – E adsorbate, where E slab, E slab+adsorbate and E adsorbate represent the energy of adsorption configure, slab and free adsorbate, respectively. Zero-damped DFT-D3 method was applied to describe the van der Waals interaction between VOCs and surface slab (Grimme et al., 2010). Projected crystal orbital Hamiltonian population (pCOHP) analysis was performed with LOBSTER program (Zhao et al., 2018).

Fig. 1 shows the XRD patterns of WO3 and WCx nanoplates. All the patterns can be indexed to the monoclinic phase of WO3 (JCPDS no. 43-1035). The orientation of WO3 or WCx nanoplates exerts great influence on the diffraction intensity. Prior to XRD measurement, the samples were placed in a sample holder and compressed to form a flat surface. The WO3 and WCx nanoplates orientated to some extent under the pressure, leading to a slightly-different diffraction intensity as shown in Fig. 1. No diffractions belonging to CDs are detected owing to their low content in WCx. Fig. 2a shows the TEM image of CDs synthesized via high-temperature reflux of sodium citrate solution. The CDs exhibit spherical shape with diameters of 2–5 nm (Fig. S1) and distinct graphite (002) planes with interplanar distance of 0.337 ± 0.011 nm (inset of Fig. 2a). The dilute CDs solution shows strong absorbance at 332 nm in the UV–vis absorption spectrum, corresponding to the n-π* transition of CO surface groups (Zhu et al., 2013). The CDs solution shows an optimal photoluminescence emission at 434 nm (inset of Fig. 2a), in accordance with previous results of graphite CDs (Sun et al., 2006).

The WO3 has a shape of quasi-square nanoplates with lateral width of 600 nm (Fig. 2b). The regular diffraction spots in the selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern proves the single crystalline nature of WO3 nanoplates (Fig. 2c). The SAED pattern can be indexed to monoclinic phase with <001> zone axis. During the SAED measurement, the WO3 nanoplate was aligned to be perpendicular to electron beam, i.e., <001> zone axis, thereby the basal planes of WO3 nanoplates can be determined to be {001}. After loading of CDs, the obtained WC1 maintained nanoplate shape (Fig. 2d). The CDs cannot be distinguished in low-resolution TEM and SEM images (Fig. S2) because of their small sizes and negligible contrast with respect to WO3. The HRTEM image (Fig. 2e) of WC1 shows the presence of monoclinic WO3 (020) and (002) lattice fringes with interplanar spacings of 0.375 ± 0.007 nm and 0.381 ± 0.006 nm, respectively. The (002) lattice fringe of graphite CDs is also observed, with a d-spacing of 0.337 ± 0.013 nm. The EDX spectrum of WC1 (Fig. 2f) proves the presence of a minority of carbon owing to the hybridization of CDs.

Fig. 2g displays the high angle annular dark field (HAADF) image of WC1 and the corresponding elemental mappings. To exclude the interference of carbon film of copper grid on the mapping of carbon element, we selected a rectangle area of a WO3 nanoplate which situated over the void of carbon film, as labeled in the HAADF image of Fig. S3. The EDX elemental mappings of C, W and O overlap with the nanoplate shape, suggesting uniform distribution of CDs on the WO3 nanoplate. Fig. 3 shows the FTIR spectra of CDs and WCx. Compared with WO3, WCx exhibits additional absorption bands of C-O (1396 ± 4 cm–1) and CO (1600 ± 4 cm–1). The oxyfunctional species originates from the surface groups of CDs, prepared via thermal decomposition of trisodium citrate. The C-O/CO bands of WCx have no wavenumber shift compared to CDs, confirming the absence of chemical bond between two phases. The Raman spectrum of CDs has characteristic D and G bands with comparable intensity (Fig. S4), indicating the presence of disordered graphitic planes. Such bands cannot be distinguished in the Raman spectra of WCx owing to the low content of CDs.

The light absorbance of WCx was explored by using a UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectroscopy. As shown in Fig. 4a, WO3 absorbs visible light with wavelengths up to 465 nm. After hybridization with CDs, the light harvesting of WO3 is enhanced slightly owing the sensitization effect of CDs. The band gap (2.46 eV) of WC1 is narrower than that (2.51 eV) of WO3 (inset of Fig. 4a). The surface areas (S BET) and pore structures of WO3 and WCx were determined by N2 sorption isotherms. The IV-type isotherms and H3-type hysteresis loops suggest the presence of slit-like nanopores (Fig. 4b). WO3 and WC1 have similar S BET (6.55 m2 g–1 for WO3 and 5.55 m2 g–1 for WC1) and pore size distribution (Table S1). Thus, the S BET and pore parameters are not the determining factor for their performance difference.

The band structure of WO3 nanoplates was determined by Mott-Schottky (M-S) plots and valence band XPS spectra. The flat-band potential (E FB) of WO3 is −0.17 V vs. NHE, estimated from the M-S plots ( Fig. 5a) (Nakajima et al., 2016). The valance band maximum (VBM) of WO3 is 2.45 eV below the Fermi level as revealed in the valence band XPS spectrum (Fig. 5b). Therefore, the potentials of VBM and conduction band minimum (CBM) of WO3 are calculated to be 2.28 V and −0.23 V vs. NHE, respectively, in terms of the band gap (Xu et al., 2018c). For WO3 nanoplates, the CBM is lower than E(O2/·O2–) (−0.046 V vs. NHE) and VBM is higher than E(H2O/·OH) (2.24 V vs. NHE) (Huang et al., 2020). As such, photoexcited electrons of WO3 can reduce O2 to produce·O2–, while photoexcited holes can oxidize H2O to yield·OH in view of thermodynamics.

The radical production of WO3 and WC1 was investigated by EPR ( Fig. 6(a-b)). WO3 shows intensive DMPO-·OH and weak DMPO-·O2– EPR signals in each spectrum, suggesting that the·OH was the dominant species produced under light irradiation. Besides, WC1 shows much higher EPR signals than WO3 in each spectrum. The enhanced signals are ascribed to the efficient separation of electron/hole pairs in WC1. We also performed active species trapping experiments to further confirm the radicals. Ethylenediaminetetracetic acid disodium (EDTA-2Na), 1,4-benzoquinone (BQ) and isopropanol (IPA) were applied as the scavengers of h+,·O2– and·OH, respectively. The photoactivity of WC1 had no apparent change upon addition of EDTA-2Na, but decreased slightly in the presence of BQ (Fig. 6(c–d)). In contrast, WC1 lost activity obviously when IPA was introduced, reaffirming that·OH was the major radical. In this work, the CBM (−0.23 V vs. NHE) of WO3 is more negative than E(O2/·O2–) (−0.046 V vs. NHE); the VBM (2.54 eV vs. NHE) of WO3 is more positive than E(H2O/·OH) (2.24 V vs. NHE) (Huang et al., 2020). Thus, O2 can be reduced by photoexcited electrons to produce·O2–, while H2O can be oxidized by photoexcited holes to yield·OH in views of thermodynamics.

The adsorption of H2O and O2 on the photocatalysts was explored by using DFT simulations. As shown in Fig. S5, CDs physically adsorbed O2 with a low E ad of −0.12 eV. Due to the weak adsorption, the production of ·O2– was kinetically inhibited since CDs were the active sites for the reaction between O2 and photoexcited electrons. In contrast, WO3 has strong chemical interactions with H2O. The surface charge of WO3 was redistributed after adsorption of H2O ( Fig. 7a). Fig. 7b shows the differential charge density (∆ρ) isosurfaces of the adsorption configure. There appear obvious electron accumulation at WO3 and electron depletion at H2O adsorbed on WO3. Further Bader charge analysis reveals that WO3 captures 0.11 electrons from the H2O molecule. The redistributed charge density activates H2O as manifested by the elongated HO bond (from 0.972 to 0.989 nm) and increased H-O-H angle (from 104.5° to 106.6°). Both favor the production of·OH on WO3 nanoplates and boost the photodegradation activity of WCx.

WO3 is the active site for the·OH production and subsequent photodegradation. We thereby explored the adsorption of HCHO and CH3COCH3 on WO3. Fig. 8(a–b) show the most stable configures of VOCs adsorbed on the WO3 (001) surface. The HCHO and CH3COCH3 are favorably adsorbed at the five-coordinated W (W5c) atoms and form WO bonds (namely, W-O linkage) with length of 2.275 and 2.228 nm, respectively. The E ad of HCHO and CH3COCH was estimated to be −1.50 and −1.86 eV, respectively, suggesting the presence of chemical adsorption of VOCs over WO3.

To explore the intrinsic interaction between WO3 and VOCs, we have performed a crystal orbital Hamilton population (COHP) analysis for the W-O linkage in each adsorption configure. The COHP is a powerful tool to indicate the bonding and antibonding characteristics of selected atomic pair in terms of their orbital-pair contributions within a periodic structure (Maintz et al., 2016). The integrated COHP (ICOHP) value is an indicative of bond strength. As shown in the projected COHP diagrams (Figs. 8c & S6), the bonding states below Fermi level overwhelm its antibonding states with an ICOHP value of −2.38 eV and −2.12 eV for CH3COCH3-WO3 and HCHO-WO3 configures, respectively, reaffirming the presence of chemical bond in the W-O linkage. We also decomposed the atomic pair interaction of the W-O linkage into individual orbital-pair contributions of W5d-O2p, W5d-O2s, W6s-O2p and W6s-O2s. Obviously, the W5d-O2p pair makes major contribution to the W-O. Such strong interaction between VOCs and WO3 correlates with the unsaturated W5c atom in the surface; the W5c atom has strong thermodynamic trend to capture oxygen of the VOC molecules and form six-coordinated [WO6] octahedron.

Work function (ϕ) is a powerful parameter indicating the electron transfer between two phases (F. Xu et al., 2019a, 2020; Liu et al., 2019; Zhao et al., 2018). We first calculated the work function (ϕ) of WO3 and CDs through DFT prediction. Fig. 9(a–b) show the electrostatic potential profiles of WO3 (001) and graphite CDs (001) slabs along their z axes, from which the ϕ values of WO3 and CDs are estimated to be 6.84 and 4.25 eV, respectively. In theory, the low-ϕ phase will donate electrons to high-ϕ phase upon their contact. As a result, some electrons in CDs would transfer to WO3 upon their hybridization until their Fermi energies reach the same level.

Such electron transfer from CDs to WO3 was evidenced by XPS analysis. Fig. 9c and Fig. S7 reveal the presence of oxyfunctional groups deriving from CDs and lattice oxygen in WO3. WO3 shows two photoelectron signals with binding energies (BEs) of 35.3 and 37.5 eV (Fig. 9d), corresponding to W 4f doublets of hexavalent tungsten (He et al., 2020; Fan et al., 2020). The W 4f7/2 binding energy (35.1 eV) of WC1 shifts negatively by −0.2 eV compared with WO3, suggesting that WO3 has captured electrons of CDs due to its larger work function (Huang et al., 2020). WO3 was negatively charged due its electron gain, while CDs were positively charged owing to their electron loss. As a result, an internal electric field (IEF) was formed at the interfaces with direction pointing from CDs to WO3. The charge density at interfaces cannot be quantified by current experimental techniques. Owing to the contact between two phases, the interphase distance (in Angstroms), instead of charge density, is the major factor to determine the IEF strength. According to previous DFT simulations, the IEF strength has the order of magnitude of 109 V m−1 (Zhu et al., 2021 ), which is strong enough to separate the charge carriers.

To explore the separation of charge carriers in WCx, we have performed an in-situ XPS measurement by collecting the photoelectron signals under 5-W LED light irradiation (365 nm). The sample was in-situ irradiated in specimen chamber during data collection. As shown in in-situ XPS spectrum of WC1 (Fig. 9d), the W 4f has a positive BE shift (~0.1 eV) with respect to that collected in dark with ex-situ XPS. The increased BE of W 4f proves that the photoinduced electrons of WO3 have transferred towards CDs (Low et al., 2019). Thus the charge carriers were spatially separated within WC1 under light irradiation.

The charge separation of WCx was further explored by using photoelectrochemical measurements and photoluminescence (PL) spectra. The zero photocurrent of WC1 recorded in dark confirms that the photocurrent comes from photoexcited electrons (
Fig. 11a). The photocurrent of WCx stabilizes within 12 s under light irradiation, which correlates with the charge transfer dynamics of photoexcited electron/hole pairs within WO3. The photocurrent of WCx increases with the CDs loading (x) and reaches the maximum value at WC1, suggesting more electrons are photoexcited and separated in WC1. Further increasing the content of CDs (e.g., WC2) leads to lower photocurrent because the CDs would shield the light absorbance of WO3. As shown in the Nyquist curves (Fig. 11b) and Table S2, WC1 has the smallest charge transfer resistance (R ct = 2196 Ω) than WO3 and other WCx. Besides, WC1 shows the weakest PL signal at 468 nm (Fig. 11c) arising from the electron/hole recombination. Both results confirmed the faster charge transfer and promising charge separation of WCx ( Xu et al., 2018a; Xu et al., 2014). We also compared time-resolved photoluminescence (TRPL) spectra of WO3 and WC1 (Fig. 11d). The decay traces were fitted to a tri-exponential decay model (Table S3). The average decay time (τ a = 2.54 ns) of WC1 is shorter than that (2.84 ns) of WO3, implying the number of photoexcited electrons of WO3 was reduced in WC1. The result suggests that some photoexcited electrons have been transferred to CDs and achieved efficient charge separation of WC1.

Based on above experimental results, we proposed a scheme to illustrate the charge separation process in WCx during the photocatalytic degradation (Fig. 10). Under visible light irradiation, the electrons in the valance band of WO3 are photoexcited and jumped to the conduction band, leaving holes in the valance band. As detailed in Section 3.4, there exists an IEF with direction pointing from CDs to WO3. Driven by the IEF, the photoexcited electrons at the conduction band of WO3 are forced to flow to CDs, while the holes are repelled and stay at the valance band of WO3. As such, the electron/hole pairs had an efficient spatial separation in WO3/CDs nanohybrids (Zhao et al., 2019). The separated charge carriers subsequently participate in surface redox and finally mineralize VOCs into CO2 and H2O.

The photodegradation performance of WCx was examined by using HCHO and CH3COCH3 as model VOCs. The VOCs cannot be degraded in absence of either photocatalysts or O2 even under light irradiation. The photocatalytic performance was described with the production rate of CO2, namely, v(CO2). The v(CO2) of WCx increases monotonically with the content of CDs ( Fig. 12a). The WC1 achieves the highest v(CO2) of 411 and 188 μmol g–1 h–1 for the degradation of HCHO and CH3COCH3, respectively. Overloading CDs (e.g. WC2) would deteriorate the performance owing to light shielding effect. We also investigated the effect of initial oxygen concertation on the photodegradation and found the photocatalytic activity increased slightly as the oxygen concentration increased from 5 vol% to pure oxygen (Fig. 12b).

Table S4 summarizes the photocatalytic performance of typical photocatalysts reported for VOCs photodegradation. Sample WC1 has a competitive advantage over other carbon-coupled photocatalysts and even shows comparable turnover frequency (TOF) with those containing noble metal cocatalysts. The recyclability of WC1 was investigated with five consecutive cycles and the v(CO2) showed no apparent decrease (Fig. S8). The spent WC1 maintained the monoclinic phase of WO3 (Fig. S9) and hexavalent tungsten (Fig. S10) after five cycling usages. The TEM image (Fig. S11) of the used WC1 still kept nanoplate-like morphology. The results proved the good recyclability of the photocatalyst.

WO3 nanoplates coupled with carbon dots (WC1) were prepared for visible light photodegradation of HCHO and CH3COCH3 with an improved v(CO2) of 411 and 188 μmol g–1 h–1, respectively, outperforming most of previously-reported photocatalysts. Electron transfer from CDs to WO3 upon their combination was confirmed by in-situ XPS and DFT calculations, which built an interfacial IEF and drove photoexcited electrons to transport to CDs but retained the holes in WO3 under visible light irradiation, achieving spatial electron/hole separation. WO3 chemisorbed gaseous VOCs, while CDs showed weak physisorption to the reactants. The hydroxide radicals (·OH) were the dominant oxidative species for VOCs degradation. Such improved photocatalytic performance of WCx was ascribed to the efficient electron/hole separation, favorable chemisorption of VOCs, and the production of·OH.

Guimei Huang and Lv Chen performed the experiments and wrote the original manuscript. Lijun Liu conducted the DFT calculations. Lingfeng Gao and Junjiang Zhu verified the analytical methods. Lijun Liu and Hongbo Fu conceived of the idea and supervised the findings of this work. All authors discussed the results and contributed to the final manuscript.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by Hubei Provincial Natural Science Foundation of China (2020CFB785), Science and Technology Research Project of the Education Department of Hubei Province (D20191703, Q20181701), Project of Hubei Key Laboratory of Biomass Fibers and Eco-dyeing & Finishing (WTU) (STRZ2020029), Special Project of the Central Committee Guides Local Science and Technology Development of Hubei Province (2019ZYYD073), and the Outstanding Young and Middle-Aged Scientific and Technological Innovation Team of the Education Department of Hubei Province (T2020011).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jhazmat.2021.127134.
